text:
<https://oxide-and-friends.transistor.fm/episodes/a-half-century-of-silicon-valley-with-randy-shoup|A Half-Century of Silicon Valley with Randy Shoup>

> Randy Shoup joined Bryan, Adam, and the Oxide Friends to look at the history of Silicon Valley through the lens of Randy's 50 years--as the child of graphics legend, Dick Shoup; an intern at Intel; aspiring diplomat; engineering leader; and father to the next generation of Shoup engineers.

elements.text: A Half-Century of Silicon Valley with Randy Shoup

Randy Shoup joined Bryan, Adam, and the Oxide Friends to look at the history of Silicon Valley through the lens of Randy's 50 years--as the child of graphics legend, Dick Shoup; an intern at Intel; aspiring diplomat; engineering leader; and father to the next generation of Shoup engineers.

text:
Want a neat programming language embedded in a Web page? How about PostScript?
<https://github.com/bellenuit/tiny-ps>

Maybe this is just nostalgia. In 1990, I wrote math code in PostScript because my printer had more computing power than my computer.

elements.text: Want a neat programming language embedded in a Web page? How about PostScript?
Maybe this is just nostalgia. In 1990, I wrote math code in PostScript because my printer had more computing power than my computer.

text: I enjoyed reading the docs for this scripting language called <https://github.com/inkle/ink/blob/master/Documentation/WritingWithInk.md|Ink> from the folks at <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Inkle_(company)|Inkle> (makers of 80 Days, Overboard, A Highland Song, and many more)

text: An update from Automat! It's now possible to use "inline assembly" to do stuff. Not very polished yet and can't do much but is pretty fast at doing that. <https://youtu.be/tjFsMnga890>

elements.text: An update from Automat! It's now possible to use "inline assembly" to do stuff. Not very polished yet and can't do much but is pretty fast at doing that.

text: I've implemented an `eval` for EYG that type checks that the evaluated program is consistent with how the evaluated result will be used in the rest of the program. To my thinking it's similar to a Type provider but implemented the same as any other effect. This allows you do hot code reloading or deserializers without an separate codegen step <https://vimeo.com/1062143358>

attachments.video_html: <iframe src="https://player.vimeo.com/video/1062143358?autoplay=1&app_id=122963" width="400" height="225" frameborder="0" allow="autoplay; fullscreen; picture-in-picture; clipboard-write; encrypted-media" title="3/3/2025, Type safe eval effect"></iframe>
attachments.fallback: Vimeo Video: 3/3/2025, Type safe eval effect
attachments.title: 3/3/2025, Type safe eval effect

elements.text: I've implemented an
eval

for EYG that type checks that the evaluated program is consistent with how the evaluated result will be used in the rest of the program. To my thinking it's similar to a Type provider but implemented the same as any other effect. This allows you do hot code reloading or deserializers without an separate codegen step

text:
<@UGWUJUZHT> re <https://open.substack.com/pub/programmingsimplicity/p/on-programming-using-non-gutenberg>, you might like this attempt of mine at introducing a graphical overview to a program:

<https://akkartik.name/2025-03-02-chessboard>

The drawback: I'm not trying to turn the diagrams into executable code. They're just documentation, but at a level that's not onerous to keep updated, then falling back on raw/eldritch textual code for the heavy lifting.
